news_summary: A once-thought-extinct plant has been rediscovered, bird populations are gaining appreciation, historic activism helped avert ecological damage, and rising gas prices are boosting public transit use.
news_summary_fr: Une plante que l'on croyait disparue a été redécouverte, les populations d'oiseaux font l'objet d'une attention croissante, le militantisme historique a permis d'éviter des dégâts écologiques, et la hausse des prix de l'essence favorise l'utilisation des transports en commun.
news_summary_es: Se ha redescubierto una planta que se creía extinta, se está prestando cada vez más atención a las poblaciones de aves, el activismo de antaño ayudó a evitar daños ecológicos y el aumento de los precios de la gasolina está impulsando el uso del transporte público.

articles.title: Plant believed extinct for 60 years suddenly reappears
articles.summary: A random photo snapped in the Australian outback has led to the rediscovery of a plant thought extinct for nearly 60 years — proving that ordinary people with smartphones are quietly transforming science. After bird bander Aaron Bean uploaded pictures of a strange shrub to iNaturalist, botanist Anthony Bean immediately recognized it as Ptilotus senarius, a rare species missing since 1967.
articles.content:
A plant believed to have vanished decades ago has been rediscovered in remote northern Australia, thanks to a chance observation uploaded to the citizen science platform iNaturalist. Scientists say the remarkable find highlights how everyday people are becoming increasingly important to modern biodiversity research and conservation.

The discovery began when Aaron Bean, a professional horticulturalist who was helping band birds on a large outback property in Queensland, noticed an unusual plant growing in the landscape. He photographed it and later uploaded the images to iNaturalist after regaining phone service.

That simple upload set off an extraordinary chain of events.

Among the millions of observations shared on the platform, the photos eventually caught the attention of botanist Anthony Bean from the Queensland Herbarium. He immediately recognized the species as Ptilotus senarius, a rare plant that had not been documented since 1967 and was widely considered extinct in the wild.

Anthony Bean had actually described the species himself a decade earlier.

"It was very serendipitous," said Thomas Mesaglio from the UNSW School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, who documented the rediscovery for the Australian Journal of Botany.

"Aaron Bean is an avid iNaturalist user who opportunistically took some photos of a few plants that were interesting on the property."

Rare Australian Plant Rediscovered

Ptilotus senarius is a delicate shrub with purple pink flowers that resemble small feathered fireworks. The species grows only in rugged terrain near the Gulf of Carpentaria in northern Australia.

Before this rediscovery, no confirmed sightings had occurred for nearly 60 years. Scientists believed it may have joined the roughly 900 plant species that have disappeared from the wild globally since the 1750s.

With Aaron Bean's photographs, Anthony Bean's expertise, and help from the property owner in collecting a specimen, researchers were finally able to confirm that the species still survives. Rather than being classified as extinct, the plant has now been moved to the critically endangered list, allowing scientists and conservation groups to focus on protecting it.

"It's one of these situations where everything had to fall into place and there was a bit of good fortune involved," Mesaglio said.

How Citizen Science Is Changing Research

The rediscovery is part of a growing pattern in science. Increasingly, members of the public are photographing plants and animals they encounter and uploading them to online databases such as iNaturalist. In some cases, these observations are revealing species thought to be lost. In others, they are helping scientists identify organisms completely new to science.

For researchers like Mesaglio, citizen science platforms have become essential tools.

Australia's enormous size and biodiversity make it impossible for scientists to survey every region themselves. Access can be even more difficult because about one third of the continent consists of privately owned land.

"If you are the property owner or you're someone who has permission from the owner to be there then suddenly it opens up this whole new world," Mesaglio said.

Scientists Want Better Biodiversity Data

Researchers are now encouraging more people, especially landowners, to participate in citizen science projects and collect high quality observations.

In New South Wales, the Land Libraries project run by the state government's Biodiversity Conservation Trust provides training and equipment to help landowners document wildlife and plant species on their properties and upload the information to citizen science platforms.

Mesaglio supports expanding these kinds of programs, both because they improve scientific access to remote or private areas and because they help build public interest in conservation.

"Engaging landholders themselves with science and the natural world and getting them more passionate about diversity makes them far more likely to be interested and invested in protecting that diversity," Mesaglio said.

Tips for Using iNaturalist

Mesaglio says detailed observations are especially valuable to scientists.

For example, a single close up image of a flower may not be enough to identify a species if many related plants have similar looking blooms. Taking additional photos of leaves, bark, stems, or the entire plant can provide critical clues.

He also encourages users to include information that may not appear in photos, such as soil conditions, nearby vegetation, or whether pollinators were present.

Even details like how a plant smells can help researchers determine what species it is.

"The more information you can provide and the more context you can provide, the more potential uses that that record will have in the future."

In separate research, Mesaglio found that iNaturalist had already been cited in scientific papers involving 128 countries and thousands of species, underscoring the platform's growing role in global science.

With millions of observations continuing to pour in, scientists believe there are many more discoveries waiting to be uncovered.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the rediscovery of a plant species thought extinct for nearly 60 years, a significant positive event for biodiversity conservation. It highlights the impactful role of citizen science in modern research, showing broad societal and environmental benefits. The story is focused, detailed, and illustrates a meaningful breakthrough in conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: Rare Australian plant thought extinct for 60 years found again
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A small shrub named Ptilotus senarius was believed to have disappeared from the wild nearly 60 years ago. Scientists thought it was extinct because no one had seen it since 1967. This plant grows only in a remote area of northern Australia, making it hard to find.

**What happened** – Aaron Bean, a horticulturalist helping with bird banding in Queensland, spotted a strange plant and took photos of it. He uploaded the pictures to an online platform called iNaturalist, where people share nature observations. Botanist Anthony Bean saw the photos and recognized the plant as the lost Ptilotus senarius. With help from the landowner, scientists collected samples and confirmed the plant still exists.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it changes the plant’s status from extinct to critically endangered. It shows how everyday people using smartphones and citizen science platforms can help scientists find rare species. It also helps conservation groups focus on protecting this plant before it disappears.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope more people, especially landowners, will use tools like iNaturalist to share nature photos. Training programs are being offered to help people collect better information about plants and animals. This will help scientists monitor biodiversity and find other rare or unknown species.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A chance photo by a nature enthusiast led to the rediscovery of a rare Australian plant missing for decades, showing how citizen science can aid conservation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une plante australienne rare, considérée disparue depuis 60 ans, retrouvée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un petit arbuste appelé Ptilotus senarius était considéré comme disparu à l’état sauvage depuis près de 60 ans. Les scientifiques pensaient qu’il était éteint car personne ne l’avait vu depuis 1967. Cette plante pousse uniquement dans une région reculée du nord de l’Australie, ce qui la rend difficile à trouver.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Aaron Bean, un horticulteur aidant à la baguage des oiseaux dans le Queensland, a remarqué une plante étrange et en a pris des photos. Il a téléchargé les images sur une plateforme en ligne appelée iNaturalist, où les gens partagent des observations de la nature. Le botaniste Anthony Bean a vu les photos et a reconnu la plante comme le Ptilotus senarius perdu. Avec l’aide du propriétaire du terrain, les scientifiques ont collecté des échantillons et confirmé que la plante existe toujours.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle change le statut de la plante, passant d’éteinte à en danger critique. Elle montre comment des personnes ordinaires utilisant un smartphone et des plateformes de science participative peuvent aider les scientifiques à retrouver des espèces rares. Cela aide aussi les groupes de conservation à protéger cette plante avant qu’elle ne disparaisse.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que davantage de personnes, surtout les propriétaires terriens, utiliseront des outils comme iNaturalist pour partager des photos de la nature. Des programmes de formation sont proposés pour aider à collecter de meilleures informations sur les plantes et les animaux. Cela aidera les scientifiques à surveiller la biodiversité et à découvrir d’autres espèces rares ou inconnues.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une photo prise par un passionné de nature a permis de redécouvrir une plante australienne rare disparue depuis des décennies, montrant comment la science participative peut aider à la conservation.
articles.personality_title_es: Planta australiana rara, considerada extinta por 60 años, encontrada de nuevo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un pequeño arbusto llamado Ptilotus senarius se creía desaparecido en la naturaleza desde hace casi 60 años. Los científicos pensaban que estaba extinto porque nadie lo había visto desde 1967. Esta planta crece solo en una zona remota del norte de Australia, lo que dificulta encontrarla.

**Qué pasó** – Aaron Bean, un horticultor que ayudaba a anillar aves en Queensland, vio una planta extraña y tomó fotos de ella. Subió las imágenes a una plataforma en línea llamada iNaturalist, donde la gente comparte observaciones de la naturaleza. El botánico Anthony Bean vio las fotos y reconoció la planta como la perdida Ptilotus senarius. Con ayuda del dueño de la tierra, los científicos recolectaron muestras y confirmaron que la planta aún existe.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque cambia el estado de la planta de extinta a críticamente en peligro. Muestra cómo personas comunes usando teléfonos inteligentes y plataformas de ciencia ciudadana pueden ayudar a los científicos a encontrar especies raras. También ayuda a los grupos de conservación a proteger esta planta antes de que desaparezca.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que más personas, especialmente propietarios de tierras, usen herramientas como iNaturalist para compartir fotos de la naturaleza. Se están ofreciendo programas de capacitación para ayudar a recolectar mejor información sobre plantas y animales. Esto ayudará a los científicos a monitorear la biodiversidad y descubrir otras especies raras o desconocidas.

**Frase clave** – Una foto casual de un aficionado a la naturaleza llevó al redescubrimiento de una planta australiana rara perdida por décadas, mostrando cómo la ciencia ciudadana puede ayudar a la conservación.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, softly glowing purple-pink Ptilotus senarius flower with feathery petals emerging vibrantly from rugged northern Australian red soil, surrounded by subtle, abstract silhouettes of a camera and a digital network web symbolizing citizen science connection, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones with gentle brushstrokes.

articles.title: Birds get a bad rap: why we should look up to our feathered friends
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01555-zMany avian species are threatened, but conservation efforts and birds’ high intelligence offer hope.
articles.content:
The Return of the Oystercatcher: Saving Birds to Save the Planet Scott Weidensaul W. W. Norton (2026)

A Bird’s IQ: Innovation, Intelligence, and Problem Solving in the Avian World Louis Lefebvre, transl. Pablo Strauss Greystone (2026)

How did birds evolve? The answer is wilder than anyone thought

Birds are in trouble. In the United States alone, one-third of bird species are rated as of high or moderate conservation concern. North American forests have lost more than one billion birds in the past half-century. A 2019 study found that grassland bird populations have declined by 53%, and 90% of those losses come from just 12 of the most common avian families, including sparrows, blackbirds and finches (K. V. Rosenberg et al. Science 366, 120–124; 2019).

Naturalist Scott Weidensaul, the author of more than 30 books about birds and nature, calls this trend “a gut punch”. Weidensaul wrote about the wonders of bird migration and the challenges facing migrating birds at a time of rapidly changing climate and massive habitat loss in his 2021 book A World on the Wing. After a colleague suggested that he might write another book about what is going right for birds, Weidensaul responded with his latest work, The Return of the Oystercatcher.

Weidensaul begins the book with the bad news, by taking the reader through a history of escalating threats to birds. Sports hunting of raptors such as eagles and falcons and the shooting of many species for their plumage, used to adorn hats in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, decimated bird populations and drove many species close to extinction. Through legislation, some destructive practices were banned and bird populations began to rebound slowly.

Bald eagles (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) were hunted nearly to extinction in the twentieth century, but conservation efforts have caused the population to make a remarkable recovery.Credit: Smithlandia Media/Getty

But a larger threat loomed: habitat destruction. Coastal development has robbed shorebirds of their nesting grounds, and the destruction of marshes, mudflats and other wetlands has deprived waterfowl of the places where they foraged. Millions of acres of grassland have been converted to agriculture, all but eliminating the native short-grass prairies that birds such as the sage grouse (Centrocercus urophasianus) depend on. Deforestation has also eliminated bird habitats in many parts of North America.

Conservation successes

The good news is that these losses have spurred a wide array of US conservation projects, which Weidensaul reports on. Thanks to government and private-sector efforts, bald eagles (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) have made a spectacular recovery since the 1970s, going from a low of 417 breeding pairs in 1963 to an estimated 324,000 birds today. He also writes of an effort to reintroduce Atlantic puffins (Fratercula arctica), a cold-loving species, to Eastern Egg Rock in the Gulf of Maine — a project that helped to inspire hundreds of seabird restoration efforts worldwide. There are now around 175 puffin breeding pairs on the rock, despite rising ocean temperatures pushing the fish that the puffins depend on into deeper waters that are difficult for the birds to reach.

Puffins (Fratercula arctica) were completely eradicated from Eastern Egg Rock in the Gulf of Maine, but conservation efforts have successfully reintroduced the species to the area.Credit: Scott Suriano/Getty

Weidensaul also relates the remarkable recoveries of two previously threatened species: piping plovers (Charadrius melodus) and oystercatchers (Haematopus palliatus). The birds had been under pressure from the growing number of people flocking to New England shores with their dune buggies and off-lead dogs, which were destroying nesting sites. Massachusetts, in the face of fierce opposition from residents, responded in the early 1990s with restrictions that helped to protect the beleaguered birds during their early-summer breeding season. Other states followed suit.

Tracing pollution in the lives of Arctic seabirds

Mountain plovers (Anarhynchus montanus)and sage grouse found unlikely allies on the Great Plains, where some conservation-minded ranchers in coalition with government agencies have helped these once-common birds to recover. And the habitats of critically endangered Hawaiian birds such as ‘a’o (the Newell’s shearwater) and ‘ua’u (the Hawaiian petrel) are being fenced off to keep predators out, helping them to hang on to a precarious existence in upland forests.

Weidensaul ends his book on a hopeful note. He’s especially encouraged, he says, by efforts in the Northwest Territories and Hudson Bay in Canada. There, Indigenous people, in partnership with the Canadian government, are setting aside vast tracts of the their homelands, the boreal forests, for conservation. If birds are to be saved, the author argues, people need to take a page from these efforts and forge personal relationships with the land that they inhabit and the creatures that live there.

Avian smarts

Another ornithologist has good news of a different sort — namely that birds are smarter than we think and remarkably resilient when responding to the challenges that they face. Birds have had a bad rap, according to Louis Lefebvre, the author of A Bird’s IQ. When we say that someone is ‘bird-brained’, Lefebvre writes, we are implying that they are not too bright. But that’s unfair, he says, not just to the humans who’ve been disparaged, but to our feathered friends, who are frequently much more clever than people give them credit for.

Lefebvre, who has spent decades conducting research on bird intelligence, argues that some brainy avians are remarkably similar in their cognitive capacity to primates such as ourselves. This is especially true of corvids, a resourceful group of perching birds that includes ravens and crows. “By evolutionary descent we may be naked apes,” the author writes. “But by virtue of our resilient, invasive, and opportunistic intelligence, we are in many ways featherless crows.”

Crows and other corvids are some of the most innovative bird species.Credit: NPL/Alamy

Lefebvre cites naturalist Charles Darwin, who held the view that human intelligence differs in degree but not in kind from that of other animals. Many highly intelligent creatures exist on distinct branches of the tree of life from Homo sapiens and evolved their brain power independently.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant conservation successes for multiple threatened bird species, demonstrating measurable positive impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems. It discusses broad, meaningful efforts involving government, private sectors, and Indigenous partnerships to restore bird populations and habitats, providing hope for environmental recovery. The focus is clear and detailed on avian conservation and intelligence, emphasizing large-scale ecological benefits.
articles.personality_title: Bird populations are bouncing back thanks to strong conservation efforts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Birds in North America have faced serious challenges in recent decades. Many species have declined due to hunting, habitat loss, and climate change. For example, grassland birds have dropped by over half, and some common birds like sparrows and finches are disappearing fast.

**What happened** – Conservation projects across the US and Canada have helped several bird species recover. Bald eagles, once nearly extinct, have grown from just 417 breeding pairs in 1963 to over 300,000 birds today. Atlantic puffins have been successfully reintroduced to the Gulf of Maine, and protected nesting areas have helped shorebirds like piping plovers and oystercatchers survive. Indigenous groups and governments in Canada are protecting large forest areas to support bird habitats.

**Impact** – These efforts show that with dedication and cooperation, it is possible to reverse damage to bird populations. The recovery of species like bald eagles and puffins is a clear sign that protecting habitats and limiting harmful activities can work. Birds also display surprising intelligence and adaptability, which helps them survive in changing environments.

**What's next step** – Conservationists hope to expand these programs and encourage more people to connect with nature and protect birds locally. Continued habitat protection, pollution control, and community involvement will be key to helping more species recover. Research into bird intelligence may also guide better ways to support their survival.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Careful conservation and respect for nature are helping many bird species recover, proving that people can make a positive difference for wildlife.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les populations d'oiseaux reprennent vie grâce à de solides efforts de conservation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les oiseaux d'Amérique du Nord ont connu de graves difficultés ces dernières décennies. De nombreuses espèces ont décliné à cause de la chasse, de la perte d'habitat et du changement climatique. Par exemple, les oiseaux des prairies ont diminué de plus de la moitié, et certains oiseaux communs comme les moineaux et les pinsons disparaissent rapidement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des projets de conservation aux États-Unis et au Canada ont aidé plusieurs espèces d'oiseaux à se rétablir. Les pygargues à tête blanche, presque disparus, sont passés de seulement 417 couples nicheurs en 1963 à plus de 300 000 oiseaux aujourd'hui. Les macareux atlantiques ont été réintroduits avec succès dans le golfe du Maine, et la protection des zones de nidification a aidé des oiseaux côtiers comme les pluvier siffleurs et les huîtriers à survivre. Des groupes autochtones et des gouvernements au Canada protègent de vastes zones forestières pour soutenir les habitats des oiseaux.

**Impact** – Ces efforts montrent qu'avec de la détermination et de la coopération, il est possible d'inverser la perte des populations d'oiseaux. La récupération d'espèces comme le pygargue à tête blanche et le macareux est un signe clair que protéger les habitats et limiter les activités nuisibles fonctionne. Les oiseaux montrent aussi une intelligence et une capacité d'adaptation surprenantes, ce qui les aide à survivre dans des environnements changeants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les conservationnistes espèrent étendre ces programmes et encourager plus de personnes à se connecter à la nature et à protéger les oiseaux localement. La protection continue des habitats, le contrôle de la pollution et l'implication communautaire seront essentiels pour aider plus d'espèces à se rétablir. La recherche sur l'intelligence des oiseaux pourrait aussi guider de meilleures méthodes pour soutenir leur survie.

**Phrase clé** – Une conservation attentive et le respect de la nature aident de nombreuses espèces d'oiseaux à se rétablir, prouvant que les humains peuvent avoir un impact positif sur la faune.
articles.personality_title_es: Las poblaciones de aves se recuperan gracias a fuertes esfuerzos de conservación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las aves en América del Norte han enfrentado grandes desafíos en las últimas décadas. Muchas especies han disminuido debido a la caza, la pérdida de hábitat y el cambio climático. Por ejemplo, las aves de pastizales han caído más de la mitad, y algunas aves comunes como los gorriones y los pinzones están desapareciendo rápidamente.

**Qué pasó** – Proyectos de conservación en Estados Unidos y Canadá han ayudado a recuperar varias especies de aves. Las águilas calvas, casi extinguidas, han pasado de solo 417 parejas reproductoras en 1963 a más de 300,000 aves hoy. Los frailecillos atlánticos han sido reintroducidos con éxito en el Golfo de Maine, y la protección de áreas de anidación ha ayudado a aves costeras como los chorlos y los ostreros a sobrevivir. Grupos indígenas y gobiernos en Canadá están protegiendo grandes áreas forestales para apoyar los hábitats de las aves.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos muestran que con dedicación y cooperación es posible revertir el daño a las poblaciones de aves. La recuperación de especies como las águilas calvas y los frailecillos es una señal clara de que proteger los hábitats y limitar actividades dañinas funciona. Las aves también muestran una inteligencia y adaptabilidad sorprendentes, lo que les ayuda a sobrevivir en ambientes cambiantes.

**Próximo paso** – Los conservacionistas esperan expandir estos programas y animar a más personas a conectarse con la naturaleza y proteger las aves localmente. La protección continua del hábitat, el control de la contaminación y la participación comunitaria serán claves para ayudar a más especies a recuperarse. La investigación sobre la inteligencia de las aves también puede guiar mejores formas de apoyar su supervivencia.

**Frase clave** – La conservación cuidadosa y el respeto por la naturaleza están ayudando a muchas especies de aves a recuperarse, demostrando que las personas pueden hacer una diferencia positiva para la vida silvestre.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant coastal scene featuring a proud bald eagle soaring above a rocky shore where colorful Atlantic puffins nest on Eastern Egg Rock, alongside a pair of piping plovers and an oystercatcher tending to their nests on sandy dunes, all surrounded by gently restored wetlands and grasslands under a soft, natural sky.

articles.title: How Russians used to protest and managed to prevent some ecological disasters
articles.summary: Russian stories of civic resistance show that environmental justice often begins with a simple question: who has the right to decide the fate of the places people call home?
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight series, “Global crisis, local solutions.” This series offers stories of resistance and successful climate action, insight into how communities in the Global South are fighting back against the crisis, analysis of what this might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

Environmental problems often seem too vast for ordinary people to confront: waste management reforms, water pollution, industrial development. This is why resistance almost always begins with something concrete — a river, a forest, a village, a road — as protecting nature becomes a way of protecting one’s home, health, and right to participate in decisions that directly shape everyday life.

In Russia, some citizens are pushing back against projects they believe have been threatening their water, land, and quality of life.

Trout farming on the river

In the spring and summer of 2025, residents of the northern Karelian town of Kem learned about plans by local authorities and the state-owned enterprise Glavrybvod to establish trout farming sites on the Kem River. According to local media reports, the proposed site was located just a few kilometers upstream from the town’s water intake facility — a location residents feared could seriously affect drinking water quality.

Karelia is Russia’s leading trout-producing region, accounting for roughly half of the country’s farmed trout production and around 10 percent of its total fish output. But the industry’s economic benefits come with environmental costs: fish farming waste can pollute lakes, rivers, and coastal waters. For years, residents across the region have protested the expansion of aquaculture projects.

For local activists opposing the project, the issue was never an abstract anti-business campaign. It was about something immediate and tangible: public health, drinking water, and quality of life. Residents say that even now, water from household taps is often little more than what they describe as “technical water,” unsuitable for safe consumption. According to Karelia’s regional branch of Rospotrebnadzor, Russia’s consumer safety watchdog, only 62 percent of the republic’s residents had access to safe drinking water in 2024.

A petition against the trout farm gathered 750 signatures, and residents eventually succeeded in drawing the attention of local authorities. In the autumn of 2025, during a public meeting attended by municipal officials and company representatives, locals voiced their opposition to the proposed fish farming sites on the Kem River. Shortly afterward, the company withdrew its application, citing a “tense situation.”

The outcome may not be final: the project could reappear later in a different form. But the conflict in Kem illustrates how environmental disputes often become about more than pollution or water quality alone. They also raise a broader question: why are decisions about the places where people live so often made without their participation?

Landfills and local resistance

In 2023, authorities in the Sysert district of Russia’s Sverdlovsk region approved plans for Yekaterinburg-Yug, a major waste management facility that would include a landfill and a waste-sorting complex. Local residents were outraged: the site was planned near residential areas and, they argued, could affect air, soil, and water quality while creating additional risks because of nearby peat deposits.

Residents of Sysert did more than organize rallies and pickets. Activists filed lawsuits, appealed to state agencies, and repeatedly attempted to initiate a local referendum. In June 2025, authorities and investors abandoned the construction plans, and in March 2026 the project was officially scrapped.

The victory did not come easily. Residents spent years building a campaign that combined legal action, public visibility, and sustained public pressure.

Battle against a landfill in northern Russia

For many participants in similar environmental movements, an important precedent was the 2018 protest in Shiyes, a remote railway station in Russia’s Arkhangelsk region that became the site of one of the country’s most high-profile environmental conflicts.

Authorities planned to build a massive landfill near Shiyes to receive waste transported from Moscow. The issue quickly grew beyond a local infrastructure dispute. For many residents, it became a symbol of the unequal distribution of environmental burdens between Russia’s political center and its regions. The movement around Shiyes took on not only an environmental dimension, but also a political one: people were defending forests and land while demanding respect for regional communities and their right to have a say in decisions affecting them.

Local residents, activists, and volunteers organized a round-the-clock protest camp, patrols, and mass demonstrations. What began in July 2018 as a local protest had, by 2019, evolved into the center of a broader anti-landfill movement. Solidarity actions in support of Shiyes took place in 30 regions across Russia.

In June 2019, construction of the landfill was temporarily suspended. A month later, on July 18, the European Parliament adopted a resolution calling on Russia to stop persecuting environmental activists in the Arkhangelsk region.

The landfill project was officially scrapped in 2020, and according to local authorities and media reports, the site was later designated for environmental restoration.

Victories in environmental conflicts rarely look dramatic. More often, they take the form of a permit being revoked, a project removed from a regional development plan, or an application quietly withdrawn. Yet such bureaucratic decisions can fundamentally reshape reality: once a project disappears from official documents, it no longer threatens a particular river, village, or forest.

Local voices, larger politics

These stories matter not because they prove that protests always succeed — that would be too simple, and not entirely accurate — but because they show that local communities can exert influence when facing bureaucratic systems, powerful business interests, and official indifference.

Environmental movements become more powerful when they are rooted in lived experience. The stories of Kem, Sysert, and Shiyes show that environmental justice often begins with a simple question: who has the right to decide the future of the places people call home?

Even where individuals may seem powerless, their voices gain strength through solidarity, persistence, and a community’s determination to defend its land, water, and future.

However, the political climate of recent years has profoundly changed the conditions for any form of protest — including local movements centered on landfill projects, deforestation, or industrial construction. Today, participation even in small local campaigns increasingly requires personal courage. For this reason, it remains unclear what forms environmental activism in Russia will take in the future.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on successful local environmental protests in Russia that prevented ecological disasters such as water pollution and landfill construction, demonstrating tangible positive impacts on public health and environmental quality. It highlights meaningful civic resistance with broad significance for communities' rights to influence decisions affecting their environment. The detailed examples provide substantive context about grassroots activism leading to real policy changes and environmental protection.
articles.personality_title: Russian communities stop harmful projects to protect water and land
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Russia, people have been facing environmental problems like pollution and waste projects that could harm their homes and health. These problems often feel too big for individuals to solve, but local communities have found ways to fight back.

**What happened** – In recent years, residents in places like Kem, Sysert, and Shiyes protested against plans for trout farms, landfills, and waste facilities that threatened their water and land. For example, in Kem, people stopped a trout farming project near their water supply. In Sysert, residents prevented a large waste facility from being built close to their homes. In Shiyes, a remote area, a major landfill project was canceled after long protests.

**Impact** – These local protests protected drinking water, forests, and the environment from pollution and risks. They also showed how people can influence big decisions when they work together. The successes helped keep communities healthier and made sure their voices were heard in important choices about their land.

**What's next step** – Even though these projects were stopped, some might return in new forms. The future of environmental activism in Russia is uncertain because protesting has become more difficult, but communities remain determined to protect their homes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Russian communities have successfully stopped harmful projects by coming together to protect their environment and health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des communautés russes stoppent des projets nuisibles pour protéger l'eau et la terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Russie, les habitants font face à des problèmes environnementaux comme la pollution et des projets de déchets qui pourraient nuire à leur maison et leur santé. Ces problèmes semblent souvent trop grands pour être résolus par des individus, mais les communautés locales ont trouvé des moyens de résister.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Ces dernières années, des résidents de Kem, Sysert et Shiyes ont protesté contre des projets d’élevage de truites, de décharges et d’installations de gestion des déchets menaçant leur eau et leurs terres. Par exemple, à Kem, les habitants ont stoppé un projet d’élevage de truites près de leur source d’eau. À Sysert, ils ont empêché la construction d’une grande installation de déchets proche de leurs maisons. À Shiyes, un projet de décharge a été annulé après de longues protestations.

**Impact** – Ces protestations locales ont protégé l'eau potable, les forêts et l'environnement contre la pollution et les risques. Elles ont aussi montré que les gens peuvent influencer de grandes décisions en s’unissant. Ces succès ont aidé à garder les communautés en meilleure santé et à faire entendre leur voix dans les choix importants sur leurs terres.

**Prochaine étape** – Même si ces projets ont été arrêtés, certains pourraient revenir sous d’autres formes. L’avenir de l’activisme environnemental en Russie est incertain car protester est devenu plus difficile, mais les communautés restent déterminées à protéger leur foyer.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les communautés russes ont réussi à stopper des projets nuisibles en s’unissant pour protéger leur environnement et leur santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Comunidades rusas detienen proyectos dañinos para proteger agua y tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Rusia, las personas enfrentan problemas ambientales como la contaminación y proyectos de residuos que podrían dañar sus hogares y salud. Estos problemas parecen demasiado grandes para que los individuos los resuelvan, pero las comunidades locales han encontrado formas de resistir.

**Qué pasó** – En los últimos años, residentes de Kem, Sysert y Shiyes protestaron contra proyectos de crianza de truchas, vertederos y plantas de gestión de residuos que amenazaban su agua y tierra. Por ejemplo, en Kem, la gente detuvo un proyecto de crianza de truchas cerca de su fuente de agua. En Sysert, los residentes impidieron la construcción de una gran planta de residuos cerca de sus casas. En Shiyes, un proyecto de vertedero fue cancelado después de largas protestas.

**Impacto** – Estas protestas locales protegieron el agua potable, los bosques y el medio ambiente de la contaminación y riesgos. También demostraron que las personas pueden influir en grandes decisiones cuando se unen. Estos éxitos ayudaron a mantener la salud de las comunidades y a que sus voces fueran escuchadas en decisiones importantes sobre sus tierras.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque estos proyectos fueron detenidos, algunos podrían regresar en nuevas formas. El futuro del activismo ambiental en Rusia es incierto porque protestar se ha vuelto más difícil, pero las comunidades siguen decididas a proteger sus hogares.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Las comunidades rusas han detenido con éxito proyectos dañinos al unirse para proteger su medio ambiente y salud.
articles.image_prompt: A serene river gently flowing through a northern Russian village, with vibrant trout swimming upstream near a clear water intake structure, while silhouettes of raised hands and petition papers subtly merge into the riverbanks, symbolizing community resistance and protection of their water and land.

articles.title: Gas prices are rising. So is public transit ridership.
articles.content:
Higher gas prices are bringing some Americans back to public transit.

The increase in ridership comes as the war in Iran has disrupted oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, pushing the national average price of gasoline beyond $4.50 per gallon. In California, drivers are paying more than $6.15 per gallon on average.

Rising fuel prices have historically pushed at least some Americans toward buses and trains, particularly commuter rail. But experts caution that decades of car-oriented development and inconsistent transit funding still leave most people with few practical alternatives to driving.

For those reasons, ridership is rising most sharply in places with robust transit systems and steep fuel prices.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

California is a clear example. Transit agencies in San Diego, Los Angeles County, and the San Francisco Bay Area have seen ridership jump in recent weeks. The San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency –– which, like others in California, received an emergency loan from the state in February –– saw its highest ridership totals since the pandemic in March.

Mark Olson, a spokesman for the San Diego Metropolitan Transit System, said gas prices probably drove the 6.5 percent jump in ridership it experienced in March compared to the previous year. Until the agency surveys riders, however, that remains an educated guess.

“A lot of our riders are low-income, and certainly gas prices can be much more sensitive to lower-income residents and riders,” Olson said. In an effort to court riders, the agency, which faces a $500 million budget deficit over the next four years, has launched a commute calculator that compares the cost of driving and public transit.

Michael Roccaforte, a spokesman for the San Francisco MTA, said it is too early to link higher gas prices to ridership increases but called the return of riders to Muni — which has undergone speed and reliability upgrades in recent years — “a promising sign.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“It’s a service that really matters to everyone here in San Francisco,” he said.

The ridership gains aren’t limited to California. The Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority in the Washington, D.C., region and Valley Metro in Texas also reported increases. Intercity passenger rail operators Amtrak and Brightline have seen a boost, too.

The trend mirrors past research showing that sustained increases in fuel costs can push some people toward public transit. Hiroyuki Iseki, an urban studies and planning professor at the University of Maryland, co-authored a study on how gasoline prices affected public transit in 10 cities between 2002 and 2011. He found that when gas prices climbed 10 percent over the course of 13 months, light rail ridership increased by 1.2 percent and bus ridership by 0.8 percent.

Iseki’s study also found psychological effects as gas prices passed different thresholds. For example, when gas prices rose by 10 percent and topped $3 per gallon, ridership of all forms of mass transit increased by about 1.2 percent. A 10 percent increase that pushed prices beyond $4 led to a 9.3 percent jump for light rail.

“Usually the people who use commuter rail take rail only for commuting, just one round trip between home and their work location,” Iseki said. “Commuter rail, the travel distance is longer than other transit trips, so the longer the distance of travel the more pricey the gasoline cost.”

Some people are better positioned to leave their cars at home, said UCLA urban planning professor Michael Manville. Those with access to commuter rail, which tends to be time competitive with driving, might make a change. But the more likely outcome is people continue driving to work and make shorter or fewer trips or even cut back on other expenses, he said. That’s because of the cognitive hurdle often required to make a switch to mass transit.

“It’s one thing to say, ‘Look, I’m just not going to drive quite as much as I used to,’ in a discretionary way,” Manville said. “It’s quite another for the typical person to then say, ‘I’m not gonna drive to work. I’m gonna figure out how the bus works.’”

There is a societal challenge as well. The U.S. has since the end of World War II made cars the focal point of city planning. “We made a bunch of policy decisions that turned them into bad masters, but they are also good servants,” Manville said of automobiles. “You throw the family in them, and you don’t have to worry about the chaos of your kids and all their stuff.”

A fundamental shift from car travel to public transit would require better-funded systems that offer greater reliability and convenience. Transit has accounted for less than a third of federal transportation funding since 1956. As of 2017, 87 percent of trips in the U.S. were taken by car.

Federal policy has an enormous impact on who does and does not have access to something like commuter rail. Elisa Ramirez, who works on policy for Transportation for America, would like to see the federal government treat mass transit as a core priority with consistent funding. Until that happens, car travel will likely continue to be the dominant mode of transport.

“Time is money, and even though people can afford a $2 fare, they can’t afford to be late for work or miss doctors appointments,” she said. “For most Americans, driving is not optional, and that’s my big thing. How much does gas impact people moving to transit? First we need to have reliable transit.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a meaningful increase in public transit ridership driven by rising gas prices, which has positive environmental implications by potentially reducing car usage and emissions. The impact is broad, affecting multiple U.S. regions and transit systems, and the article provides detailed context on the challenges and significance of this shift.
articles.personality_title: Rising gas prices lead more Americans to choose public transit
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gas prices in the United States have been rising, with the national average going above $4.50 per gallon and prices in California exceeding $6. This increase is partly due to disruptions in oil shipments caused by the war in Iran.

**What happened** – As fuel costs go up, more people in several U.S. cities are using public transportation such as buses, trains, and commuter rails. Transit agencies in places like San Diego, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Washington D.C., and Texas have reported higher ridership in recent weeks. For example, San Diego’s transit system saw a 6.5 percent increase in riders in March compared to last year.

**Impact** – This rise in public transit use shows that many people respond to higher gas prices by finding alternatives to driving. Experts note that this effect is strongest where transit systems are reliable and fuel costs are high. Studies also show that when gas prices cross certain levels, more people switch to transit, especially for longer trips like commuting. However, challenges remain because many American cities were built for cars, and public transit often lacks consistent funding and convenience.

**What's next step** – Transit agencies are working to improve services, like making buses faster and more reliable, to attract even more riders. Experts say that for a bigger shift away from cars, the government needs to invest more regularly and heavily in public transit. Without better funding and service, many people will still find driving easier or necessary.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Higher gas prices are encouraging more Americans to use public transit, especially where systems are strong, but lasting change depends on better transit funding and service.
articles.personality_title_fr: La hausse des prix de l'essence pousse plus d'Américains vers les transports en commun
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les prix de l'essence aux États-Unis augmentent, avec une moyenne nationale dépassant 4,50 $ le gallon et des prix en Californie supérieurs à 6 $. Cette hausse est en partie due aux perturbations des livraisons de pétrole causées par la guerre en Iran.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Avec la hausse des coûts du carburant, davantage de personnes dans plusieurs villes américaines utilisent les transports en commun comme les bus, les trains et les trains de banlieue. Les agences de transport à San Diego, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Washington D.C. et au Texas ont signalé une augmentation du nombre de passagers ces dernières semaines. Par exemple, le système de transport de San Diego a vu une hausse de 6,5 % des utilisateurs en mars par rapport à l'année précédente.

**Impact** – Cette augmentation de l’utilisation des transports en commun montre que beaucoup de gens réagissent à la hausse des prix de l’essence en cherchant des alternatives à la voiture. Les experts notent que cet effet est plus fort là où les systèmes de transport sont fiables et où le carburant est cher. Les études montrent aussi que lorsque les prix de l’essence dépassent certains seuils, plus de personnes utilisent les transports, surtout pour les trajets plus longs comme les déplacements domicile-travail. Cependant, des défis subsistent car beaucoup de villes américaines ont été conçues pour la voiture, et les transports publics manquent souvent de financements réguliers et de commodité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les agences de transport travaillent à améliorer leurs services, comme rendre les bus plus rapides et plus fiables, pour attirer encore plus d’usagers. Les experts disent que pour un changement plus important, le gouvernement doit investir de manière régulière et importante dans les transports publics. Sans de meilleurs financements et services, beaucoup de personnes continueront de trouver la voiture plus facile ou nécessaire.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La hausse des prix de l’essence encourage plus d’Américains à utiliser les transports en commun, surtout là où les systèmes sont solides, mais un vrai changement dépend de meilleurs financements et services.
articles.personality_title_es: El aumento del precio de la gasolina impulsa a más estadounidenses a usar el transporte público
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los precios de la gasolina en Estados Unidos han subido, con un promedio nacional que supera los 4,50 dólares por galón y precios en California que superan los 6 dólares. Este aumento se debe en parte a interrupciones en los envíos de petróleo causadas por la guerra en Irán.

**Qué pasó** – A medida que aumentan los costos del combustible, más personas en varias ciudades estadounidenses están usando el transporte público, como autobuses, trenes y trenes de cercanías. Las agencias de transporte en San Diego, Los Ángeles, San Francisco, Washington D.C. y Texas han reportado un aumento en la cantidad de pasajeros en las últimas semanas. Por ejemplo, el sistema de transporte de San Diego tuvo un aumento del 6,5 % en marzo en comparación con el año anterior.

**Impacto** – Este aumento en el uso del transporte público muestra que muchas personas reaccionan a los precios más altos de la gasolina buscando alternativas a conducir. Los expertos señalan que este efecto es más fuerte donde los sistemas de transporte son confiables y el combustible es caro. Los estudios también muestran que cuando los precios de la gasolina superan ciertos niveles, más personas usan el transporte, especialmente para viajes largos como el trayecto al trabajo. Sin embargo, existen desafíos porque muchas ciudades estadounidenses fueron diseñadas pensando en los autos y el transporte público a menudo carece de financiamiento constante y comodidad.

**Próximo paso** – Las agencias de transporte están trabajando para mejorar los servicios, como hacer que los autobuses sean más rápidos y confiables, para atraer a más pasajeros. Los expertos dicen que para un cambio mayor, el gobierno debe invertir de forma regular y significativa en el transporte público. Sin mejor financiamiento y servicios, muchas personas seguirán encontrando que conducir es más fácil o necesario.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El aumento de los precios de la gasolina está animando a más estadounidenses a usar el transporte público, especialmente donde los sistemas son fuertes, pero un cambio duradero depende de un mejor financiamiento y servicio.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a bustling cityscape featuring stylized, colorful buses and trains smoothly flowing along tracks and roads, with softly glowing gas pumps in the background showing rising prices, all rendered in natural, muted tones to symbolize the shift from car reliance to growing public transit use amid rising fuel costs.
